We first developed and validated the novel 20-item Cringe Scale to measure individual differences in trait cringe which we found positively predicted state cringe perceptions, incentivized donations, and behavioral intentions. Additionally, we found that cringe, as compared to vicarious embarrassment, was elicited more intensely by intentional and public norm violations, and was associated with heightened moral wrongness and punishment judgements as well as reduced empathy and prosocial motivation toward the transgressor. Path analyses revealed that perceptions of transgressor intent and norm violation severity mediated these differences. Together, our results establish cringe as a functionally distinct variety of vicarious embarrassment characterized by psychological distancing rather than empathic concern. The findings inform our understanding of an increasingly important self-conscious emotion and illuminate the adaptive social functions of cringe in social cognition.

articles.title: Effectiveness of a School-based Workshop Promoting Mental Health on Adolescents Mental Health Literacy and Emotional Self-Efficacy
articles.authors: Joana Rupprecht; Tina Wendel; Salka Beck; Markus Grill; Matthias Ziegler; Bettina K. Doering; Anke Haberkamp
articles.abstract:
Theoretical background: Mental health crisis in adolescents is a growing concern in society and research. Because of the high prevalence of mental disorders during adolescence and the long waiting times for access to therapeutic services, low-threshold, school-based prevention programs are needed that empower adolescents to actively advocate for their mental health. The aim of this study was to assess the effectiveness of a one-day school-based mental health workshop on mental health literacy (MHL) and emotional self-efficacy (ESE) within a quasi-randomized delayed cross-over design. 

Method: Data was collected from 868 students (age: 14-17 years, 39.3% female) in six schools between March 2024 and February 2025 at three data assessment points. Participants in the intervention group (n1 = 365 (M₁ = 14.8 (0.86), 54.8% female) participated in a one-day school-based mental health workshop provided by the organization Kopfsachen e.V. between the baseline-assessment (T0), 2-3 weeks before the workshop and the post-assessment (T1) directly after the workshop. There was a follow-up assessment (T2) around six months after the baseline (T0). Participants in the waitlist-control group (n2 = 503 (M₂ = 15.0(0.85)) participated at all three assessments and received the workshop after the follow-up assessment (T2).  

Results: The workshop had significant short-term effects (T0-T1) on all MHL and one ESE variable within the overall group comparison, with the strongest effect on professional help-seeking-intentions (β = 0.42, p <.001, η²ₚ = .04). However, these effects were not sustained until the follow-up assessment (T3). Explorative Exploratory analyses revealed significant moderating effects of gender, age, personal experience, circumstantial experience, and personal relevance. Qualitative data revealed that the needs and expectations of the adolescents before the workshop largely matched to their take-home messages after the workshop. 

Conclusions: This study confirms the short-term effectiveness of a one-day school-based mental health workshop on adolescents’ MHL and ESE. Fading effects over time underscore the need for tailored interventions, booster sessions, and continued research to ensure sustainable long-term impact.

articles.title: Patterns of registered and self-reported sleep problems in young adults with different histories of childhood adversity
articles.authors: Jette Echterhoff; Tjeerd Rudmer Vries; Adrian G. Zucco; Katrine Strandberg-Larsen; Levente Kriston; Naja Hulvej Rod
articles.abstract:
Background: Sleep problems are increasing among young adults, with those experiencing childhood adversity particularly at risk. This study provides a comprehensive overview of sleep problems in young adults following multifaceted trajectories of childhood adversity, while emphasizing the transdiagnostic nature of sleep problems.

Methods: Register data on 1,276,831 individuals were supplemented with nested survey data on 36,873 participants. Childhood adversity (ages 0-16) was operationalized using previously derived trajectories: low adversity, early life material deprivation, persistent material deprivation, loss or threat of loss, and high adversity. Sleep problems (ages 16-30) were assessed through prescriptions, diagnoses, and self-reported data. Survival models were used to estimate associations between childhood adversity and sleep problems. Co-occurrence of sleep and mental health problems were explored, and confirmatory factor analysis was used to model self-reported sleep impairment across adversity groups.

Findings: Childhood adversity was associated with sleep problems, with prevalences of 8∙2%, 10∙4%, 10∙8%, 13∙6%, and 20∙1% across the five adversity groups. Risk was highest in the high adversity (HR 2∙37; 95% CI 2∙32–2∙43) and loss or threat of loss (1∙71; 1∙68–1∙74) groups vs. low adversity. Sleep problems most commonly involved sleep medication prescriptions only (49%) but also co-occurred with mental disorders (43%). The high adversity and loss or threat of loss groups showed higher variation in self-reported sleep impairment and higher proportions above the 80th percentile compared with other groups.

Interpretation: Sleep problems are common among young adults who experienced childhood adversity; linking early-life stress, disturbed sleep, and mental health conditions from childhood into adult life. These interconnected public health challenges underscore the relevance of multi-sectoral policy approaches, including upstream prevention of childhood adversity and downstream recognition and treatment of sleep problems, while considering variability in how these problems develop, manifest, and persist.

Funding: Lundbeck Foundation (grant no. R396-2022-352) and Tryg Foundation (grant no. 157232).

articles.title: “It Feels Like We are an Outsider”: Understanding Barriers and Creating Opportunities for Fathers’ Engagement in Parenting Programs
articles.authors: Carolina Garraio; Dora d'Orsi; Marc Grau-Grau; Paula Mena Matos; Marisa Matias
articles.abstract: Becoming a father entails significant psychological adaptations that impact fathers’ well-being and mental health, but also that of their partners and children. As such, structured parenting programs may play an important role in supporting fathers during this transition. However, fathers remain underrepresented in these programs, and studies consistently report challenges in recruiting and engaging them. These difficulties suggest a potential misalignment between parenting programs and fathers’ needs, expectations, and sociocultural contexts, often shaped by their broader experiences with family- and health-related services. This study aims to explore how Portuguese fathers perceive and position themselves in relation to parenting programs, and to identify the social, cultural, and institutional factors that shape their engagement and non-engagement. We conducted four focus groups with 16 fathers who had at least one biological child under five years old. The focus groups were moderated by two psychologists (one male and one female) and followed a semi-structured script. Using reflexive thematic analysis, six themes were developed: fathers as secondary parents – experiences and perceptions of exclusion; generational gaps; gap between service awareness and willingness to engage; perceived legitimacy of fathers in parenting support spaces; seeking a place of belonging; design elements supporting fathers’ engagement. Overall, fathers’ engagement emerges as a contextually embedded process shaped by institutional practices, cultural expectations, and perceived legitimacy. Enhancing participation may entail fostering inclusive environments that recognize fathers as central caregivers. This calls for broader transformations of cultural and institutional frameworks that shape parenting support from the earliest stages of a father’s parenthood trajectory.

articles.title: How and when to implement self-efficacy interventions to maximize the benefits of virtual reality exposure therapy for fear of heights
articles.authors: Kayleigh Piovesan; Julia Klenke; Armin Zlomuzica
articles.abstract: Strategies aimed to enhance self-efficacy (SE), such as mastery experiences and verbal persuasion may promote exposure therapy. Here, we evaluated the effects of two strategies to boost SE administered during and/or after virtual reality exposure therapy (VRET) for fear of heights. Height-fearful participants (N = 83) were randomly assigned to one of four conditions: VRET alone, VRET combined with SE enhancement via positive performance feedback during VRET, VRET combined with post-exposure SE via mastery experience activation and evaluation, or VRET combining both SE enhancement strategies. Subjective, behavioral, and psychophysiological indices of height fear and general SE were assessed from pre- to post-intervention and at 3-month follow-up. Across conditions, height fear and avoidance decreased while behavioral approach increased with effects largely maintained at follow-up. Participants receiving post-exposure mastery experience activation and evaluation showed the strongest reductions in subjective fear during the behavioral approach test and in anxiety expectancies. Decrease in subjective height avoidance was also stronger compared to the condition that received SE enhancement during VRET. Notably, participants receiving positive performance feedback during VRET showed the least improvement (across several outcome measures). Our results indicate that post-exposure activation and evaluation of mastery experiences is suitable to further promote VRET outcome in height fear. Conversely, applying positive performance feedback during VRET seems ineffective and leads to diminished exposure treatment benefit. These findings provide novel perspectives on the potential use of SE enhancement as a supplementary strategy to augment exposure therapy. This study was preregistered on ClinicalTrials.gov (ID: NCT05824884).

articles.title: Psychometric Validation of a Deep Learning-based Foreground Speech Detection Algorithm for Everyday Conversation Detection
articles.authors: Amanda Marie Bernal; Johannes Leonhard Klinz; Valeria Pfeifer; David Sbarra; Charles L Raison; Nicole Nugent; Rajat Hebbar; Shrikanth Narayanan; Matthias R. Mehl
articles.abstract: Passive ambient audio sampling bears great potential for objectively measuring daily social activity and its association with wellbeing and health. However, detecting true conversations via human annotation is labor-intensive, and automatic labeling via audio signal processing has thus far only received proof-of-concept validation. Here, we conduct a comprehensive psychometric validation of a deep learning-based foreground speech detection algorithm for conversation activity detection (CAD) from ambient audio sampled with the Electronically Activated Recorder (EAR) method (Hebbar et al., 2021). We assess the CAD algorithm’s validity as an objective measure of conversation activity using four archival EAR datasets with human ground-truth conversation annotations (N = 566 participants, n = 167,539 audio recordings). Specifically, we evaluate, across the four samples, the degree to which the CAD algorithm converges with human-annotated conversation activity, yields temporal stability estimates of conversation activity, and replicates patterns of external correlates (with demographic, wellbeing, and personality measures) comparable to those derived from human ground-truth annotations. We further compare the distributional properties of conversation activity derived from the CAD algorithm and human ground-truth annotations and use this information for thresholding the algorithm’s continuous conversation activity estimates. Overall, the CAD algorithm evidences strong psychometric properties for estimating conversation activity across a range of participants and study characteristics, suggesting that it is suitable for at-scale deployment to objectively measure daily socializing from passively sampled ambient audio.

articles.title: Social cognition, imitation, and sensory processing difficulties in extremely preterm-born children at six years of age
articles.authors: Ulla Niutanen; Marjo Metsäranta; Piia Lönnberg; Elina Wolford; Eeva Aronen; Aulikki Lano
articles.abstract: This study compared children born extremely preterm (EPT, before 28 weeks of gestation) with full-term children (FT) on social-cognitive abilities, sensorimotor function, socioemotional behavior, and sensory processing. Fifty-three EPT and 34 FT children aged 6 to 7 underwent standardized assessments of Affect Recognition, Theory of Mind, and Imitating Hand Positions, and parents completed validated questionnaires. EPT children showed weaker theory-of-mind abilities, greater difficulty with imitation, and more sensory processing problems than their FT peers. The findings highlight the need for research to understand the mechanisms behind these difficulties and to develop targeted interventions to improve long-term outcomes for EPT children.

articles.title: Mapping social knowledge structures: A large-scale, data-driven approach to uncovering clinically relevant person profiles that shape social learning
articles.authors: Yen-Wen Chen; Josue Garcia; Isabella Grace Mark; Christoph Korn; Gabriela Rosenblau
articles.abstract: Clinical symptomatology is reflected in social dimensions, but how these dimensions are structured and vary across individuals remains unclear. Here, we set out to examine core social dimensions underlying self-reported personality traits and item preferences. We leveraged these dimensions to derive latent person-level profiles, which explained meaningful variability in clinical symptomatology. Moreover, we show that generalizations of item- and person-level social information and associated clinical symptoms can be used as social knowledge structures across various social learning and decision-making contexts. In Study 1, a final sample of 1,529 participants completed extensive self-report measures of personality traits and item preferences, which were organized into six trait and four food preference dimensions based on Exploratory Graph Analysis (EGA). These EGA dimensions were used to specify person-level profiles with Latent Profile Analysis (LPA). The resulting profiles demonstrated external validity, generalizing to independent measures of personality and clinical symptoms. Notably, we identified a “Maladaptive Trait profile” and a “Raw-Produce-Aversion/Texture-Sensitive preference profile,” both associated with external measures such as elevated autistic traits and lower emotional stability, greater behavioral rigidity, and lower emotional stability. In Study 2, we assessed the robustness and generalizability of these dimensions and profiles in an independent sample of German-speaking participants (N=649). The dimensional structure showed partial replication across samples with preservation of core dimensions. The “Maladaptive Trait profile” demonstrated the strongest cross-sample correspondence, whereas broader food preference profiles replicated more consistently than narrower ones. Study 3 demonstrates how item dimensions and people profiles can be used to probe social learning. Social learning performance differed depending on the person profile in question. Together, this work establishes a unified, data-driven framework of item-level structure and person-level groupings that capture the samples’ considerable variability in clinical symptoms. This framework provides a way to investigate social learning and decision-making–key processes that are highly relevant to psychopathology across a wide range of clinical disorders.

articles.title: Learning about ‘Inflation’: Mapping Caregivers’ Mentalizing and Pedagogical Strategies that Support 8-9-year-old Children’s Learning of Abstract Concepts
articles.authors: Dora Szegedi; Gal Rozic; Peter Fonagy; Antonia Hamilton; Gabriella Vigliocco
articles.abstract: Understanding how children learn abstract concepts is critical for developmental theory and pedagogy, yet how caregivers support this process remains poorly understood. We examined semi-naturalistic interactions in 30 caregiver–child dyads (English-speaking children aged 8–9 years, 70% girls and 30% boys), in which caregivers (66% female, 34% male) explained 10 abstract financial concepts (e.g., inflation). Data was collected in London, UK between 2024 and 2025. Caregivers’ speech was annotated using a novel qualitative codebook. Generalized mixed- effects models indicated that three strategies were associated with higher odds of immediate and delayed learning: checking common ground (“Have you heard about inflation?”), using child- centered second-person hypotheticals (“Imagine you go to the store, and milk is now £2 instead of £1.”), and implicit comprehension checks (“When is there inflation?”). In contrast, personal examples (“Remember dad’s favorite chocolate was 75p...now it’s £1.35!”) were associated with lower odds of learning, suggesting a hindering effect of loading the child with salient but concept-irrelevant information. These findings highlight the role of perspective-taking and attention to children’s knowledge state in supporting abstract concept learning. Limitations include the use of English and a highly educated sample.

articles.title: Identifying and repairing mismatches between metadata and actual participant identifiers in CHILDES
articles.authors: Trevor K. M. Day
articles.abstract: Child Language Data Exchange System (CHILDES) is a large corpus of transcribed child speech covering many languages. In this brief analysis, I describe mismatches between participant identifiers between direct access to CHILDES and the R package childesr. I provide a file to correctly re-link childesr data to actual participant identifiers.

articles.title: Preferences for speech governance are polarized across 40 countries
articles.authors: Tobia Spampatti; Laura K. Globig; Steve Rathje; Jay Joseph Van Bavel; Hallgeir Sjåstad
articles.abstract: The dilemma of balancing freedom of expression against limiting the spread of misinformation defines speech governance in today's information ecosystems. Yet, we lack cross-cultural, systematic evidence on how citizens prefer to solve this dilemma and why. To measure citizens’ preferences for speech governance, we surveyed people in 40 countries across ten world regions (N = 47,719; representative for age, gender, and political ideology). People are nearly evenly split between protecting free expression (51%) and preventing misinformation from spreading (49%). However, we find that this preference is politically polarized: in 32 out of 40 countries, right-leaning participants prioritize protecting free expression more than left-leaning participants (55% vs. 48%). This polarization pattern is amplified by political interest: The more interested in politics participants are, the more right-leaning participants support free speech and left-leaning participants want to prevent misinformation from spreading. These results showcase how difficult it is for people across the world to find balancing free speech against reducing misinformation. Moreover, these results also identify what could be a new cleavage of ideological polarization that has direct implications for governing expression in information ecosystems.

articles.title: "AI Psychosis” as folie à 1.1:AI-induced delusion and trust in Large Language Models
articles.authors: Jakob Ohlhorst
articles.abstract: The advent of chatbot applications for LLMs like ChatGPT has led to a novel psychiatric phenomenon popularly called “AI psychosis”. Excessive LLM-use appears to trigger in the user the acquisition of delusions about the LLM, the user, or the world. More appropriately called AI-induced delusion, I argue that this phenomenon is not as novel as it appears. Rather, it parallels the already known phenomenon of folie à deux or induced delusion where a person with a delusion communicates and transmits their delusion to another person in a close, isolated social relationship. A key social epistemological mechanism for folie à deux is the deep trust in these close relationships. There is a philosophical debate about whether trust in AI systems is even possible, given that they are no agents. The phenomenon of AI-induced delusion shows that trust in LLMs is indeed possible. However, these delusions result from a conflation of interpersonal and mere pragmatic trust. These and other harms resulting from the conflation of different senses of “trust” means that we should nevertheless stop talking of “trust” in the context of extant AI systems in order to avoid this harmful confusion.

articles.title: Why Some Words Stick and Others Fade: A Neurocognitive Perspective on Vocabulary Learning
articles.authors: Manuela Macedonia; Brian Mathias
articles.abstract: Research on word learning and memory often treats words as interchangeable, overlooking how linguistic features shape encoding and recall. In this review, we advance a neurocognitive-linguistic perspective on word memorability that integrates behavioral, linguistic, and neuroscience evidence to explain why some words “stick” while others fade. We synthesize findings on how familiarity, frequency, concreteness, emotional valence and arousal, phonological structure, and semantic distinctiveness influence word learning and retention. We link these features to several underlying neural mechanisms—including amygdala-hippocampal coupling, sensorimotor cortical engagement, and inferior frontal and anterior temporal hubs—and situate them within predictive and embodied frameworks of language. These insights help to refine theories of word memorability and carry practical implications. We connect basic mechanisms to educational practice (evidence-based vocabulary selection, gesture- and rhythm-based scaffolding, adaptive digital learning) and to clinical rehabilitation (aphasia and dementia interventions that leverage high-imageability, emotionally meaningful or dense-neighborhood items, and multimodal encoding).  Finally, we argue for integrating linguistic and neurocognitive determinants into computational and theoretical models of word learning to improve predictions of memorability and support more personalized approaches to language instruction and rehabilitation.

articles.title: How to Measure Machiavellianism? A Psychometric Evaluation of Six Scales
articles.authors: Michael P. Grosz; Julia Fuchs; Mitja D Back; Christian Blötner
articles.abstract: Recently, several new Machiavellianism (Mach) scales have been developed to overcome psychometric issues of classical scales such as the Mach IV (Christie & Geis, 1970). The growing number of scales has made it challenging for researchers to select an appropriate Mach measure. The current study evaluated the psychometric properties of six contemporary Mach scales—the TDMS, FFMI-SSF, MAAQ, M4, M7, and SD4 Mach— using data from 1,293 participants in the GIPO project (Grosz et al., 2025). We tested their factor structures using the simulation-cum-ROC approach for model fit cutoffs, assessed reliability estimates via McDonald's ω, examined measurement precision using graded response models, and investigated construct and criterion validity through correlations with self-report measures and incentivized behavioral measures of cheating and prosocial behavior. Expert expectations about correlation magnitudes were collected and used as benchmarks for equivalence testing and the quantifying construct validity procedure. None of the six proposed factor structures met the simulation-derived model fit cutoffs. Across reliability and validity analyses, the M4 emerged as the most psychometrically favorable scale overall, combining the highest omega total (.90) and IRT-based marginal reliability (.88) with good congruence with expert-expected correlation patterns. The TDMS and MAAQ are recommended as alternatives for researchers interested in the two-dimensional structure of Mach, as both exhibited acceptable performance in validity and reliability analyses. The FFMI-SSF warrants caution: The nomological networks of the Agency and Planfulness subscales diverged substantially from expert expectations for Mach. The present findings provide a foundation for more informed measurement selection in empirical Mach research.

articles.title: Trade-off between performance and human-like perception in face recognition models
articles.authors: Sobhan Hojjati; Dina Keshvari Ghadikolai; Alireza Shakeripour; Zahra Bahmani Dehkordi; Kazuhisa Shibata; Hakwan Lau; Ali Moharramipour
articles.abstract: Deep learning models for face recognition are widely adopted in cognitive neuroscience, yet it is not well understood whether their excellent performance means they “see” faces like humans. Here, we collected a relatively large dataset of human face-similarity judgments to assess how well prominent face recognition models align with human perception. We found that models with high — but not the highest — recognition performance are often best aligned with human similarity judgments. We further tested to what extent a linear transformation could better align model representations with human perceptual similarity. Models with superior recognition performance benefited the least from this transformation, suggesting a deeper mismatch with human perception. Notably, the transformation reduced recognition performance for higher-performing models but gave low-performing models a slight recognition boost. Overall, our results indicate that a trade-off exists between recognition ability and human-like perceptual similarity. This may inform future work on developing more human-like models.

articles.title: Modeling Working Memory Capacity in Sentence Processing with Lossy-Context Surprisal
articles.authors: Jiaxin Li; Michael Hahn; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract:
Understanding how comprehenders interpret linguistic input in real time has long been a focus of sentence processing research. 
The recently-proposed Resource-Rational Lossy-context Surprisal (RR-LCS) model can be applied for making predictions at the level of individual readers: it predicts that individuals with low working memory capacity (WMC) have a lossy representation of the previous context and may therefore experience high surprisal at a long-distance dependency, while individuals with high WMC will experience a lower surprisal effect as this dependent to them may still be highly predictable from previous context. To date, these predictions have not been empirically tested. Here, we evaluate them using a controlled A-Maze task (Experiment 1) and an analysis of reading more naturalistic text based on the InDiCo corpus (Experiment 2). In Experiment 1, we investigated how WMC influences sentence processing across short, long, and very long dependency conditions. The results revealed that low-WMC participants initially showed a strong locality effect between the short and long conditions, which decreased over time due to adaptation. High-WMC participants demonstrated locality effects between both the short-long and long-very long conditions from the start, with these effects gradually diminishing as their memory allocation became more efficient. These findings align with the RR-LCS model's predictions, showing that high-WMC individuals maintained larger context sizes, such that they could more easily process long-distance dependencies, whereas low-WMC individuals, constrained by smaller context sizes, experienced pronounced locality effects. Experiment 2 confirmed that WMC modulates sensitivity to lossy-context surprisal (LCS), with LCS improving model fit particularly strongly for low-WMC participants.  These findings underscore the significance of individual differences in WMC for processing complex syntactic structures. By combining experimental data with computational modeling, we provide a deeper understanding of how WMC shapes language comprehension.

articles.title: Intuitive judgments of strangers are more popular but not more accurate than deliberate ones
articles.authors: Carina Remmers; Albert Anoschin; Johannes Zimmermann; Michael P. Grosz; Mitja D Back; Richard Rau
articles.abstract: Popular advice in media and self-help literature encourages people to trust their intuition when forming impressions of strangers. We tested this widespread lay belief by examining whether intuitive judgments are preferred over deliberate ones and whether such preferences are justified by differences in accuracy. In Study 1 (n = 401), participants from the general population reported using intuition more frequently, preferring it, and expressing greater confidence in it compared to deliberate reasoning when judging strangers. In Study 2, we analyzed 21,739 judgments from 380 users of the quiz app “Who Knows,” who inferred personality-related characteristics from short video introductions. Judgment mode was experimentally manipulated within persons across multiple game rounds. While intuitive judgments were again preferred, accuracy did not differ between conditions. Thus, although intuition is favored when judging strangers, it does not yield higher accuracy but may still be adaptive by achieving comparable accuracy with less time.

articles.title: Adaptation and Validation of the Spanish version of the Perceived Occupational Stress Scale: Evidence from Factor Structure, validity, and Nomological Network
articles.authors: David Saeteros; María Villaplana García; Guillermo Soriano-Tarrín; Adriana Trujillo; Laura Viñals-Vilà; Juan Carlos Onieva; David Gallardo-Pujol
articles.abstract:
Work is a double-edged sword. It fosters well-being but it is also linked to stress and burnout.  Recognizing that stress is ubiquitous and best understood encompassing both the objective and subjective perspective, this study focuses on evaluating the psychometric properties of the Spanish adaptation of the Perceived Occupational Stress Scale (POSS). In Study 1, we translated and back-translated the POSS and administered it to 675 Spanish individuals. The Spanish POSS showed strong internal reliability (α = 0.903) and a good fit with the proposed factor structure (CFI = 0.946, RMSEA = 0.206, SRMR = 0.031). Study 2 explores the impact of faking on participants' responses using an experimental design. Our findings underscore the significance of work-related factors, such as mistreatment and lack of appreciation, in predicting faking behavior. In Study 3, we delved into the nomological network of the POSS by examining correlations with related psychological variables. Notably, we only found positive correlations between perceived occupational stress and measures of work engagement and psychosocial factors.
Overall, the Spanish POSS demonstrates strong validity and reliability, closely mirroring the original scale. As an EU-compliant tool, it effectively assesses perceived occupational stress, enhancing organizational support evaluations and deepening our understanding of occupational well-being.

articles.title: Why faster thinkers are smarter thinkers: Working memory binding as the link between processing speed and cognitive ability
articles.authors: Anna-Lena Schubert; Linda Eileen Leydecker; Henrike Maria Jungeblut; Mareike Hülsemann
articles.abstract: Individuals who process information faster tend to score higher on cognitive ability tests, but the mechanism linking processing speed to general cognitive ability (g) remains unresolved. We tested whether this association is mediated by working memory capacity, and if so, whether the critical component is executive attention — as captured by complex span tasks requiring dual-task coordination and interference management — or relational binding — the ability to form and maintain temporary associations between unrelated features. In a community sample of 215 adults (aged 18–40), we measured processing speed at both the neural level (ERP component peak latencies) and the cognitive level (diffusion model drift rates), working memory through complex span and binding tasks, and g through a comprehensive cognitive ability test battery. Latent variable mediation analyses showed that complex span performance only partially mediated the speed–g relationship, leaving significant direct effects. In contrast, binding task performance fully mediated the relationship for cognitive processing speed and substantially mediated it for neural processing speed, reducing previously significant direct paths to nonsignificance. When both mediators competed in parallel models, binding emerged as the dominant predictor of g, with complex span contributing no unique variance. These findings identify relational binding as the specific working memory component that transmits the association between processing speed and cognitive ability, suggesting that the cognitive bottleneck linking processing speed to reasoning ability is not executive control but the more elemental ability to build and maintain temporary relational representations.

articles.title: The use of motor imagery in geriatric rehabilitation : a case report before a cardiac surgery
articles.authors: Ambre KOMONSKI; Angele METAIS; Anne Sophie BOUREAU; France MOUREY; Thomas Rulleau
articles.abstract:
Introduction : This case illustrates the use of motor imagery in prehabilitation for an old patient with severe aortic insufficiency in the context of ischemic heart disease. Due to contraindications to preoperative exercise, no physical activity had been initiated. However, the worsening of his functional limitations led us to consider adaptations starting the preoperative phase.
Diagnosis : The patient presented with moderately impaired motor performance, including muscle weakness, cardiorespiratory deconditioning, and preserved walking ability. His motor imagery abilities were good.
Intervention and results : An intervention combining physical exercise (25%) and motor imagery (75%) was implemented during the 4 weeks prior to surgery. After surgery, a slight decline in motor performance, consistent with the documented postoperative trajectory, was noted. Adherence to the program was excellent.
Conclusion : This case demonstrates the feasibility of integrating motor imagery as a prehabilitation tool, offering a safe option for at-risk patients.

articles.title: Rebuilding HiTOP from the ground up: Symptom-level analyses and a revised mapping to the DSM
articles.authors: Miriam K. Forbes; Tam Pham; Matthew Roberts; Carly Johnco
articles.abstract: The evidence for the structure of the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP) model relies heavily on analyses of correlations among DSM diagnoses, which entrenches some of their limitations. In this study, we deconstructed the mental disorders covered in HiTOP into individual symptoms—with personality disorders represented via item-level assessment of the Alternative Model for Personality Disorders facets—and rebuilt the HiTOP model from the ground up. Participants self-selected into an online mental health study and were randomly split into primary (n = 11,762) and hold-out (n = 3,000) samples to identify a robust hierarchy of empirically derived constructs in the results, ranging from symptoms and syndromes to very broad ‘superspectra’ of psychopathology. In each sample, both hierarchical clustering and hierarchical principal components analyses were used; the final hierarchical structure was derived based on points of cross-sample and cross-method agreement. The resulting model was remarkably similar to the HiTOP model, including nearly all of the same subfactors, spectra, and superspectra—albeit with some reorganisation in the structure. The inclusion of broad hypomania/disinhibition and pathological introversion dimensions represented the most prominent differences compared to the current higher-order structure of HiTOP. The detailed lower-order constructs found here also mirrored many of the existing components and traits in HiTOP, as well as indicating some potential areas for revision. Finally, we re-mapped the disorders included in the Structured Clinical Interview for the DSM-5 onto the new model. Overall, the results suggest that the structural foundations of the HiTOP model are largely sound.

articles.title: Environmental regularities shape visual search strategy selection
articles.authors: Chris Ahn; Wouter Kool
articles.abstract: Visual search can be guided by multiple strategies, some of them relying on bottom-up salience, others on top-down expectations. However, little is known about how we arbitrate between these strategies. Here, we tested whether environmental regularities that incentivize different strategies can influence this arbitration. Participants performed a visual search task in which targets appeared in one of two color-defined groups of items whose relative sizes varied across trials. Because smaller groups are more visually salient, prioritizing them constitutes a bottom-up search strategy, and prioritizing the larger group requires top-down control. By manipulating the probability that the target appeared in each group, we created environments in which abstract structural properties of the display (the relative size of the groups) incentivized different strategies. Participants adapted their search strategy to these abstract regularities, but in an asymmetric fashion. Reliance on salient minority groups readily increased when it was advantageous, whereas people only became willing to prioritize the majority group when alternative salience-based cues were minimized. These results suggest that visual search strategies are shaped by environmental statistics, but their adoption is constrained by the cognitive costs of overriding bottom-up salience.

articles.title: Beliefs without Strong Supporting Evidence: Associations with Personality Items
articles.authors: Solana Redway; Judy Cheng; Jessica Khangura; Bryan K.S. Woodward; Abhijit Mahesh Chinchani; Jiaying Zhao; Katie M. Lavigne; Mahesh Menon; Todd Stephen Woodward
articles.abstract: Beliefs without strong supporting evidence (BWSSE), such as conspiracy theories and pseudoscientific beliefs, are increasingly common, and their growing prevalence may have negative consequences for society. The design of more effective interventions to mitigate the spread of BWSSE may depend on understanding the personality characteristics associated with different belief types. To meet this goal, this study optimized components from a BWSSE questionnaire with respect to overlap with the Big Five Aspect Scale (BFAS), which assesses personality. Data from 417 participants who completed the BWSSE questionnaire and BFAS within standard completion times were analyzed using a multivariate statistical method to identify how individual BFAS items, rather than aggregate domain scores, were associated with the BWSSE questionnaire components optimized for overlap with the BFAS. We found that New Age beliefs were associated with creativity and emotional sensitivity (primarily items from the Openness and Agreeableness domains), Science beliefs with a preference for order, routine and systematic thinking (primarily items from the Conscientiousness and Intellect domains), Traditional Religion with respect for authority and a less expansive vocabulary (primarily items from the Agreeableness and Intellect domains), and Conspiracy Theories with enjoying conflict and easily becoming angered and agitated (primarily items from the Neuroticism domain). Different belief types were associated with distinct combinations of individual BFAS items that crossed BFAS domains, highlighting within-domain dissociations that are obscured when using aggregate domain scores. These belief-personality associations, combined with reasons for holding BWSSE, should be considered when developing strategies to reduce misinformation and promote evidence-based reasoning.

articles.title: An energy-gradient theory of embedded predictions in the brain
articles.authors: Kuzma Strelnikov
articles.abstract:
measured by neuroimaging methods, and how they relate to internal predictive models during states of rest. 
By understanding these gradients, we can gain insight into how the brain efficiently processes information and adapts to environmental changes. We discuss how energy flows may underlie gradients of activity between voxels and spatial coding in fMRI and describe computational algorithms that can be implemented in brain activity images. 
We propose that the brain continuously maintains and updates predictions about the environment through internal models, which are embedded in the dynamic activity flows observed both at rest and during cognitive tasks. This perspective highlights the significance of energy turnover in supporting cognitive functions, as neural circuits reflect predictions and adjustments based on past experiences. 
We argue that the interplay between predictive processing and energy dynamics provides a richer understanding of cognitive mechanisms and informs future research directions in psychology and physiology.  These insights emphasize the importance of considering energetic principles in brain physiology.

articles.title: Reaction time cost produced by an auditory warning stimulus in mixed blocks
articles.authors: Luiz Eduardo Ribeiro do Valle; Regina Shiotuki
articles.abstract:
Several studies have demonstrated that a spatially noninformative auditory or visual warning stimulus (WS) facilitates responding to a target stimulus (TS). Results obtained recently indicated that the WS can also inhibit responding for a few hundred milliseconds. Other recent results indicated that basal responsivity is reduced in blocks including WS-absent and WS-present trials. The current study examined the possibility that a mismatch between the current trial and the previous one in terms of absence versus presence of the WS leads to a transient reduction of responsivity. In a first experiment we used an auditory WS and visual TSs, and in a second experiment, a visual WS and auditory TSs. The presentation of the auditory WS but not the visual WS reduced choice reaction time. Choice reaction time in auditory WS-absent trials was longer after a WS-present trial than after a WS-absent trial.  This result indicates a decrease of responsivity in WS-absent trials which succeed WS-present trials. The 
effect might be due to the unexpectedness of the appearance of the visual TS not preceded by the auditory WS.

articles.title: Cross-linguistic test of the pragmatic-informativeness account: Evidence from superordinate noun acquisition in Mandarin Chinese
articles.authors: Xiaotao Liu
articles.abstract: Superordinate nouns such as “animal” or “fruit” denote broad categories and carry distinctive distributional signatures in child-directed speech that learners may use to identify their meaning. On this pragmatic-informativeness account, English caregivers mark superordinate nouns with a discriminative cue inventory. The present study asks whether the account is language-general or parameterized by language-specific resources, and whether the same cues shape acquisition timing. Two studies are reported. Study 1 is a corpus replication of Choe and Papafragou (2026) on eleven Mandarin CHILDES corpora (364,417 adult utterances). Study 2 conducts an item-level age-of-acquisition (AoA) analysis in Mandarin (Xu et al., 2021) and English (Kuperman et al., 2012), linking each item’s distributional exposure in the language-appropriate CHILDES corpus to its rated AoA. Study 1 finds that Mandarin superordinates are over-represented in all four Mandarin-available critical contexts. Study 2 shows that, once corpus frequency is controlled, distributional distinctiveness does not independently predict AoA in either language; frequency alone accounts for substantial AoA variance. The cross-linguistic convergence is consistent with a shared pragmatic-informativeness pressure, but the acquisitional link is mediated primarily by input frequency.

articles.title: Designing Psychometric Measures for LLMs: Framework and Application to Racial Bias
articles.authors: Mouhacine Benosman
articles.abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI), particularly in the form of large language models (LLMs) or chatbots, has become increasingly integrated into our daily lives. In the past five years, several LLMs have been introduced, including ChatGPT by OpenAI, Claude by Anthropic, and Llama by Meta, among others. These models have the potential to be employed across a wide range of human–machine
interaction applications, such as chatbots for information retrieval, assistance in corporate hiring decisions, college admissions, financial loan approvals, parole determinations, and even in medical fields like psychotherapy delivered through chatbots. The key question is whether these chatbots will interact with humans in a bias-free manner or if they will further reinforce the existing pathological biases present in human-to-human interactions. If the latter is true, then how can we
rigorously measure these biases?
We address this challenge by introducing STAMP-LLM (Standardized Test & Assessment Measurement Protocol for LLMs), a principled two-phase framework for designing psychometric measures to evaluate chatbot biases: (i) a Definitional phase for construct mapping, item development, and expert review; and (ii) a Data/Analysis phase for protocol control (prompts/decoding), automated sampling,
pre-specified scoring, and basic reliability/validity checks. We illustrate STAMP-LLM on racial bias using one explicit and two implicit measures.

articles.title: Integrative Psychiatry: Reflections on the NIMHANS Model
articles.authors: Gokul Raj
articles.abstract:
Integrative psychiatry combines conventional psychiatric treatments with evidence-informed complementary approaches to address the biological, psychological, social, and existential dimensions of mental illness. Growing patient use of complementary therapies and limitations of conventional models have increased interest in such approaches; however, structured exposure within psychiatric training remains limited. 

This Viewpoint describes the integrative psychiatry model at the National Institute of Mental Health and Neurosciences (NIMHANS), India, and examines its clinical, educational, and research components. The model is
characterized by multidisciplinary collaboration across psychiatry, yoga, Ayurveda, and naturopathy, with joint clinical assessment and integrated treatment planning. Clinical services incorporate pharmacotherapy
alongside lifestyle modification, yoga-based interventions, and traditional therapeutic practices. Training programs include interdisciplinary teaching, supervised clinical exposure, and research engagement.

Observations from clinical training suggest enhanced patient engagement and cultural acceptability of integrative approaches. Selected studies from the department indicate potential benefits of integrated interventions,
including improvements in metabolic parameters and symptom outcomes; however, these findings are interpreted cautiously, with clear distinction between empirical evidence and clinical observation.

Key challenges include variability in the evidence base, issues of standardization, and the need for additional training and cross- disciplinary coordination. Comparison with international models highlights
opportunities for structured curriculum development and scalability. Integrative psychiatry, as exemplified by the NIMHANS model, may complement conventional psychiatric care. Further evaluation is required
to establish its effectiveness, generalizability, and role in psychiatric training.

articles.title: Mystical Experiences, Psychosis, and Psychedelics: A Case Series on Meditation and Psychedelic-Induced Altered States
articles.authors: Gokul Raj
articles.abstract:
Background: Spiritual and psychotic experiences share overlapping phenomenology, often making clinical differentiation challenging. The growing use of advanced meditation and psychedelics has further blurred this boundary. 

Methods: A retrospective case series was conducted at the Department of Integrative Medicine,of a tertiary care mental health facility in India. Four individuals presenting with meditation or psychedelic-induced altered states and Mystical Experience Questionnaire (MEQ-30) scores ≥90 were analyzed. 

Results: Four representative cases are presented: two meditation-induced psychoses, one psychedelic-associated affective psychosis, and one recurrent spiritual preoccupation with depression. MEQ-30 scores ranged from 110-138, reflecting high mystical intensity. Most patients responded well to antipsychotic medication and supportive therapy, with one showing sustained post-recovery transformation. Conclusion: Meditation and psychedelic practices can trigger psychotic or affective episodes in predisposed individuals. Screening, supervision, and integrative management are essential for prevention and recovery.

articles.title: Memory-guided selective attention: Evidence for context-specific control using the trial-unique Stroop task.
articles.authors: Stephanie Lauren Souliere; Nicholaus P. Brosowsky
articles.abstract: Stroop effects can be influenced by context-specific cues associated with varying levels of proportion congruency, even for items that occur with equal frequency in each context. This theoretically important result points to the involvement of a cue-driven memory process in the dynamic updating of selective attention. However, attempts to replicate this result have been challenging, potentially due to limitations or confounds in the experimental design, or difficulties in inducing context-specific learning due to the presence of other dominant item-specific cues. The present study aimed to re-evaluate the memory-guided hypothesis using the trial-unique Stroop paradigm, where each trial consists of a unique stimulus and response that never reappear throughout the experiment, thereby preventing trial-to-trial associative regularities. One context was associated with high conflict (20% proportion congruency), and the other with low conflict (80% proportion congruency). The primary finding indicated that trials within the low conflict context exhibited larger congruency effects compared to those in the high conflict context. These results align with the memory-guided selective attention hypothesis, suggesting that selective attention priorities may become associated with contextual cues and are automatically reinstated through a memory retrieval process.

articles.title: “Anyone Could Do This!”: Multiverse as an Undergraduate Psychology Capstone Project
articles.authors: Peter Allen; Emily Abraham; Agnes Bleach; Rory Hill; Sana Mahmud; Jordan Wild
articles.abstract:
Background: Heyman and Vanpaemel (2022) proposed “many-multiverses-one-dataset” as a pedagogically sound alternative to traditional undergraduate capstone projects. However, they did not evaluate this model. In our context, capstone projects require a higher level of individualization. Furthermore, project students value choice, autonomy, and ownership. Consequently, we have iteratively developed a variation of Heyman and Vanpaemel’s model in which students choose their own effect/dataset for an individual multiverse capstone project.  

Objective: To evaluate student-led individual multiverse capstone projects. 

Method: One cohort of five project students qualitatively reflected on designing, running, and reporting an individual multiverse project in a small group supervision context. We thematically analyzed these reflections.

Findings: Two themes captured (a) the factors promoting project success (intrapersonal, interpersonal, and contextual) and (b) the outcomes of a multiverse capstone project (the range of skills developed and multiverse’s potential contributions to psychology).

Conclusion: Individual multiverse projects are a meaningful alternative to traditional capstone projects, which do not require the collection of primary data and address many key undergraduate research methods learning outcomes.

Teaching Implications: We propose an evidence-based recipe for successful individual multiverse projects. In a post-COVID world, all project supervisors should have possibilities like this up their sleeves.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the TechTra Scales: A Tool to Assess Perceptions of and Needs for Technology Transparency in UX Research
articles.authors: Ilka Hein; Daniel Ullrich; Sarah Diefenbach
articles.abstract: The users’ subjective experience of a technology’s transparency is a crucial variable in human-computer interactions, shaping trust, satisfaction, and use. Technology transparency refers to different types of information, such as what the technology currently does, how it gets its results, and why. Depending on the use context, more or less transparency can be helpful and desired from a user experience perspective, posing a design challenge and making it important to assess and contrast both users' transparency perceptions and needs. In three studies (N1 = 191, N2 = 23, N3 = 73), covering different technologies, user groups, and methodological approaches, we developed the TechTra Scales — including TechTra-N for measuring users’ transparency need and TechTra-P for users’ transparency perception. Results support the relevance of differentiating between needs and perceptions, the scales’ applicability and sensitivity to different technology interactions, and their validity. Moreover, we showed good scale properties and a high use intention of practitioners, underlining TechTra’s significance for research and industry.

articles.title: Judgment Patterns and Decision Processes in Children’s Non-symbolic Probabilistic Choices
articles.authors: Maybí Morell-Ruiz; Federico Abraham-Ceruti; Magnus Haake; Agneta Gulz; Alejandro Maiche
articles.abstract:
Children’s probabilistic reasoning yields seemingly conflicting results across task conditions. While children often succeed when heuristic cues match probabilistic structure (congruent condition), they usually perform at chance when these cues conflict with proportional information (incongruent condition). This apparent inconsistency reflects a broader tension between probabilistic and heuristic accounts of reasoning under uncertainty, raising questions about whether judgment patterns remain consistent across conditions and whether they reflect distinct underlying decision-making mechanisms.
We studied probabilistic reasoning in six-to-ten-year-old children (N = 92) using a two-alternative forced-choice task with congruent and incongruent trials, with small or large probability differences. A Gaussian Mixture Model identified three clusters in the incongruent-large condition: a proportion-based group, a heuristic group relying on target numerosity, and a chance-level group. Distinct sensitivities to probability differences and numerosity indicated consistent judgment patterns across conditions. Diffusion modeling showed that the heuristic group had higher decision efficiency in congruent trials and a pre-decisional bias toward the jar with more targets, whereas the proportion-based group accumulated evidence more efficiently in incongruent trials and showed unbiased pre-decisional states.
These findings suggest that developmental transitions in probabilistic reasoning may not follow a linear path toward formal logic but rather involve a shifting balance between coexisting cognitive mechanisms that remain stable at the individual level throughout childhood.  This challenges a strict dichotomy between probabilistic and heuristic accounts, suggesting instead that multiple cognitive mechanisms and stable individual differences underlie the emergence of probabilistic intuition in children.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the TechTra Scales: A Tool to Assess Perceptions of and Needs for Technology Transparency in UX Research
articles.authors: Ilka Hein; Daniel Ullrich; Sarah Diefenbach
articles.abstract: The users’ subjective experience of a technology’s transparency is a crucial variable in human-computer interactions, shaping trust, satisfaction, and use. Technology transparency refers to different types of information, such as what the technology currently does, how it gets its results, and why. articles.title: Separating Cognitive Development from Language Development in the Acquisition of Negation using International Adoption
articles.authors: Annika M McDermott-Hinman; Samuel Zimmerman; Jesse Snedeker; Roman Feiman
articles.abstract: Children learning words face at least one challenge, maybe two. First, children must gather enough information to identify which concept maps to which word (a linguistic bottleneck). Second, for this mapping to be possible, children may need to acquire the concept itself if it is not already available (a conceptual bottleneck). We investigate whether children face both challenges or only the first when learning the English words for negation, a concept that is abstract and non-referential, yet universal across human thought and languages. We tested whether a linguistic bottleneck is sufficient to explain how children learn to express negation by comparing negation production in typical English-learners and older internationally-adopted preschoolers, who are more conceptually mature but face a similar language-learning task. If infants normally face a conceptual bottleneck, older adoptees, who do not, should learn negation faster. If the only bottleneck is linguistic, the two groups should learn negation at the same rate relative to their acquisition of English. In Study 1, parent reports on the CDI showed that older international adoptees and language-matched infant first-language learners begin producing no and not at the same point in vocabulary growth. Study 2 examined children’s production of logical denial uses of negation words in longitudinal corpora of parent-child interactions. Adopted preschoolers’ and language-matched infants’ production of denials increased at the same rate relative to the mean length of children’s utterances. Matching patterns of negation acquisition in internationally-adopted preschoolers and first-language-learning infants support a solely linguistic, not conceptual, bottleneck on learning negation words.

articles.title: Real-time Functional Connectivity Analysis Using Multivariate Autoregressive Model-based Independent Component Analysis in Task-related High-density EEG
articles.authors: Amir Jahanian Najafabadi; Khaled Bagh
articles.abstract: This chapter extends real-time EEG connectivity analysis beyond resting-state conditions by introducing a unified framework for online directed connectivity estimation using multivariate autoregressive independent component analysis (MVARICA). While prior work has demonstrated the feasibility of real-time connectivity in quasi-stationary resting-state data, many neuroscientific applications involve more complex temporal structures, including continuous naturalistic stimulation and task-based paradigms organized around discrete events. The present work addresses this gap by developing a context-aware framework that supports both sliding-window analysis of continuous data and event-centered comparisons relative to inter-trial intervals within a single computational pipeline. Using high-density preprocessed EEG, the framework was evaluated across continuous and task-based paradigms, with a focus on model stability, connectivity agreement between offline and online estimates, hyperparameter behavior, detection of event-related effects, and real-time latency. Results show that online partial directed coherence estimates closely match offline estimates across more than 28,000 segment-band pairs, with high correlation, low absolute error, and consistently stable model fits. Agreement exhibits systematic frequency dependence, improving from lower to higher frequency bands, and is more robust at the level of region-of-interest (ROI) representations than at the channel level. Notably, strong connectivity agreement is maintained even when hyperparameter selection differs substantially between offline and online pipelines. Event-related validation reveals that while continuous connectivity structure is reliably reproduced, detection of task-versus-baseline effects depends on spatial scale. Channel-level detection overlap is low, reflecting sensitivity to statistical thresholding, whereas ROI-level analyses show high concordance, indicating consistent identification of large-scale network interactions. Latency analysis further demonstrates that the full pipeline operates within real-time constraints, with total processing time remaining below the analysis window and computational cost dominated by source-informed decomposition. Together, these findings demonstrate that real-time, source-informed directed connectivity can be extended to temporally heterogeneous EEG paradigms without compromising interpretability or computational feasibility. The proposed framework bridges the gap between resting-state connectivity analysis and experimentally structured data, supporting both continuous monitoring and event-sensitive interpretation of neural interactions in real time.

articles.title: Associations of Psychosocial Factors with Internalizing and Externalizing Mental Health Problems Following Traumatic Brain Injury: An Application of the HiTOP-TBI Model
articles.authors: Hannah Perry; Jennie Ponsford; Alexia Samiotis; Gershon Spitz; Jai Carmichael
articles.abstract:
Objective: To examine associations between life stressors, resilience, and coping across diverse mental health problems among adults with moderate-severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) using the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology Following TBI (HiTOP-TBI) transdiagnostic model.
Setting: Community survey.
Participants: 117 adults were sampled cross-sectionally from a longitudinal cohort previously admitted for inpatient rehabilitation (74% male, M=14.72 years post-injury). 
Design: Observational, cross-sectional.
Main Measures: Comprehensive battery of psychiatric questionnaires including the Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms – Expanded Version, Somatic Symptom Scale – 8, Comprehensive Assessment of Traits Relevant to Personality Disorders, and the Externalizing Spectrum Inventory – Brief Form. This battery assessed ten factor analytically derived psychopathology constructs forming the HiTOP-TBI model, a hierarchical, transdiagnostic, and dimensional model, capturing both internalizing and externalizing psychopathology. Psychosocial factors included current life stressors (assessed via open-text), resilience (Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale), and adaptive and non-productive coping (Coping Scales for Adults). Functional disability was assessed using the World Health Organisation Disability Assessment Schedule (WHODAS 2.0)
Results: Wilcoxon rank-sum tests found life stressors were significantly associated with higher psychopathology, while Spearman’s correlations found resilience was associated with lower psychopathology, both primarily across internalizing dimensions. Spearman’s correlations found non-productive coping to be associated with higher psychopathology across both internalizing and externalizing dimensions. Adaptive coping demonstrated no significant associations. Multivariable regressions found that associations involving resilience and non-productive coping held after controlling for disability, whilst associations with life stressors did not. Regression analyses further indicated that associations between lower-order HiTOP-TBI dimensions and psychosocial factors were largely accounted for at the higher-order level (i.e., general internalizing or externalizing).
Conclusions: This study provides an integrated understanding of associations between psychosocial factors and internalizing and externalizing psychopathology after TBI, suggesting that life stressors, resilience, and coping may represent promising transdiagnostic targets for psychological rehabilitation.

articles.title: Depressive Symptom Trajectories Around the Retirement Transition Across Countries and Historical Periods
articles.authors: Yufei Zhang; Zhihao Ma
articles.abstract: As global aging accelerates, retirement has become a critical transition in older adults’ lives, with profound implications for mental health. Depression trajectories around retirement are inconsistent, with some studies suggesting worsening symptoms and others indicating improvement. This study will examine depressive symptom trajectories around the retirement transition using data from four aging studies: HRS, SHARE, CHARLS, and KLoSA. We emphasize three key components of the retirement transition: retirement age, pre-retirement phase, and post-retirement phase. Using multilevel models, we will estimate changes in depressive symptoms around retirement transition, accounting for cross-national and historical differences. The findings will contribute to a deeper understanding of how individual retirement decisions interact with macro-level factors, providing insights for the design of policies that support mental health during the retirement transition.

articles.title: Benefits of co-learning with an AI agent
articles.authors: Jacob Feldman; Lazaros Gallos; Hao Wang; Vladimir Menkov; Paul B. Kantor
articles.abstract: The advent of effective machine learning techniques raises the question of how such procedures might benefit human learning. In this paper we study how humans solve a type of classification puzzle—the Game of Hidden Rules (GOHR)—with vs. without the assistance of a “bot” that provides potentially helpful suggestions about how to proceed. In a GOHR game, the learner attempts to sort colored shapes into categories according to a hidden rule that they must discover, for example “red shapes go to bucket #0”, “blue shapes to bucket #1,” etc. In some conditions, a "bot" made suggestions, which the human learner was free to follow or ignore. Even though human learners did not always take the bot’s advice, we found a consistent performance advantage in bot conditions compared to no-bot conditions, meaning that participants solved these problems more quickly when the bot was present than when it was not. This effect was particularly pronounced in lower-performing subjects, while high-performing subjects were relatively unaffected. We also manipulated the learning speed of the bot, and found that the benefit of bot assistance increased with bot "intelligence." Our results demonstrate that bot assistance can be helpful to human learners, and shed some light on the prospects of AI-supported human learning.

articles.title: Benefits of co-learning with an AI agent
articles.authors: Jacob Feldman; Lazaros Gallos; Hao Wang; Vladimir Menkov; Paul B. Kantor
articles.abstract: The advent of effective machine learning techniques raises the question of how such procedures might benefit human learning. In this paper we study how humans solve a type of classification puzzle—the Game of Hidden Rules (GOHR)—with vs. without the assistance of a “bot” that provides potentially helpful suggestions about how to proceed. In a GOHR game, the learner attempts to sort colored shapes into categories according to a hidden rule that they must discover, for example “red shapes go to bucket #0”, “blue shapes to bucket #1,” etc. In some conditions, a "bot" made suggestions, which the human learner was free to follow or ignore. articles.title: Single-trial principal components analysis of event-related potentials: Back-projection, theoretical rationale, empirical evaluation, and reliability
articles.authors: Jürgen Kayser
articles.abstract: Temporal principal components analysis (tPCA) is widely used to identify and quantify event-related potentials (ERPs). It is less commonly applied to single-trial EEG epochs, despite concerns about the low signal-to-noise ratio and uncertainty about how single-trial components compare with those derived from averaged waveforms. Alternatively, averaged principal components (PCs) can be imposed on single trials via back-projection. This study evaluated the fidelity of single-trial and back-projected single-trial tPCA (covariance-based, unrestricted Varimax rotation) in representing interpretable averaged PCs by systematically comparing factor loadings and factor scores in two existing ERP datasets (72-channel, N=152; 67-channel, N=98), using both surface-referenced (infinity, nose) and reference-free current source density (CSD) transformations. In the 72-channel dataset with four balanced conditions (emotional hemifield paradigm), single-trial PCs closely matched those from averaged data, whereas in the 67-channel dataset with three imbalanced conditions (novelty oddball task), they did not. In contrast, across datasets and transformations, back-projected single-trial PCs consistently reproduced virtually identical averaged PCs. All single-trial tPCA solutions enabled full reconstruction of the original data matrix, confirming the methodological validity of back-projected decomposition, and critically, back-projection enabled estimation of internal consistency for PCA-derived component measures at the single-trial level, with all aggregated component scores showing high or adequate dependability. These findings suggest that single-trial tPCA may approximate averaged solutions only when the variance structure is preserved, whereas back-projection provides a robust alternative by quantifying single-trial epochs relative to the averaged covariance structure, thereby maintaining component structure, integrity, and interpretability of trial-level component estimates in continuity with established ERP constructs.

articles.title: Environmental regularities shape visual search strategy selection
articles.authors: Chris Ahn; Wouter Kool
articles.abstract: Visual search can be guided by multiple strategies, some of them relying on bottom-up salience, others on top-down expectations. However, little is known about how we arbitrate between these strategies. Here, we tested whether environmental regularities that incentivize different strategies can influence this arbitration. Participants performed a visual search task in which targets appeared in one of two color-defined groups of items whose relative sizes varied across trials. Because smaller groups are more visually salient, prioritizing them constitutes a bottom-up search strategy, and prioritizing the larger group requires top-down control. By manipulating the probability that the target appeared in each group, we created environments in which abstract structural properties of the display (the relative size of the groups) incentivized different strategies. Participants adapted their search strategy to these abstract regularities, but in an asymmetric fashion. Reliance on salient minority groups readily increased when it was advantageous, whereas people only became willing to prioritize the majority group when alternative salience-based cues were minimized. These results suggest that visual search strategies are shaped by environmental statistics, but their adoption is constrained by the cognitive costs of overriding bottom-up salience.

articles.title: No Data Left Behind - A Primer on Bayesian State-Space Models for Acceptance Sampling
articles.authors: Henrik R. Godmann; Lotte Mensink; ruud wetzels; Eric-Jan Wagenmakers
articles.abstract: Acceptance sampling is used to make accept-reject decisions on batches of products by taking a sample and evaluating the quality of the sampled items. If the number of defective items in the sample exceeds a certain threshold, the entire batch (or lot) is rejected. The most common acceptance sampling method is frequentist and based on an a priori assessment regarding quality and risk levels. This standard approach comes with a number of inherent limitations. In this manuscript, we demonstrate how these limitations can be overcome by conducting acceptance sampling with the help of a Bayesian state-space model. We apply both the traditional approach and the Bayesian state-space methodology to simulated data and illustrate the additional insights provided by the latter. The statistical background and modeling choices of the Bayesian state-space model are explained in detail. We provide accessible and user-friendly software implementations of the Bayesian state-space model in R, and in the JASP module Acceptance Sampling.

articles.title: Gadgets, niches, and distributed cognition: a proposal on the external structures of the human mind
articles.authors: Ulysses Paulino Albuquerque
articles.abstract: In this paper, I approach cognition as a way of being within something (the brain) and with something (the environment), thus exhibiting a dual nature: rooted in interactions with the world, yet not fully reducible to them. I propose expanding the notion of cognitive gadgets by introducing second-order cognitive gadgets - external, material, or symbolic structures that not only support but also shape and transform the conditions of possibility of the cognition. These second-order gadgets function as cognitive structures that actively participate in the architecture of the human mind, influencing its ontogenesis, plasticity, and epistemic regimes. By articulating this proposal with niche construction theory and abductive reasoning, I argue that cognition is not simply distributed across the mind, body, and world but emerges from cognitive agents' continuous shaping of the environment. Finally, I examine the role of artificial intelligence as a contemporary instance of a second-order gadget, whose effects are not limited to cognitive support but involve a profound reconfiguration of thought styles and collective mental ecologies.

articles.title: Belief, Evidence, and Predictions in Simple Bayesian Acceptance Sampling Plans with JASP
articles.authors: Henrik R. Godmann; Lotte Mensink; Pankaj Singla; Koen Derks; Eric-Jan Wagenmakers
articles.abstract: The standard frequentist methodology for acceptance sampling suffers from several limitations, including the inability to accommodate prior knowledge and the inability to provide a graded measure of certainty. A Bayesian analysis overcomes these limitations and yields three main outcomes of interest. Firstly, the posterior distribution reflects the current belief about the underlying defect rate in the lot; secondly, the Bayes factor grades the evidence provided by the data for competing hypotheses concerning lot defect rate;  thirdly, the posterior predictive distribution summarizes the current expectations about the total number of defectives in the lot under investigation. Together, these three outcomes provide a comprehensive perspective on acceptance sampling that we argue can usefully supplement or supplant the standard frequentist methodology. To facilitate its wider adoption we have implemented the proposed Bayesian workflow in the open-source software program JASP.

articles.title: Misleading AI guidance in psychological publishing
articles.authors: Zhicheng Lin
articles.abstract: In response to generative AI, SAGE, the Association for Psychological Science (APS), and the American Psychological Association (APA) have introduced policies to regulate its use in their journals. These policies share sensible aims—protecting confidentiality, preventing fabrication and plagiarism, preserving human accountability—but they also embed strong claims about large language models (LLMs). Such claims—often oversimplified and tied to particular deployments—anchor distinctions between “assistive” and “generative” use, set disclosure and citation rules, and justify blanket bans on entering content into AI systems. Using SAGE, APS, and APA guidance as a case study, I show how these claims propagate into policy rules and create recurring mismatches between underlying assumptions and stated requirements across six domains: capability claims, categorization, prohibited uses, confidentiality, disclosure, and attribution. Policies also disagree on basic issues such as whether routine AI‑assisted copyediting must be disclosed. I propose a mechanism‑ and risk‑based policy matrix that organizes AI use by material contribution to the scientific record and confidentiality risk, yielding four editorial stances. Applying this framework to APS and APA policies produces technology-agnostic language that preserves editorial aims while reducing disclosure burden, improving cross-journal coherence, and accommodating the heterogeneous deployments and workflows that capability-specific rules cannot stably track.

articles.title: Awareness and Adoption of Open Research Practices in Ireland: A Nationwide Survey Analysis
articles.authors: Dounia Lakhzoum; Ciara Egan; Denis O'Hora; Ann-Marie Creaven; Chris Noone; Karen Matvienko-Sikar; Fran Callaghan; Darren Dahly; Ellen Breen; Niamh
articles.abstract: In response to the reproducibility crisis, substantial and targeted efforts have promoted Open Research Practices (ORPs) to ensure research is rigorous, reliable and robust. To assess current needs, and following similar efforts in the UK and the Netherlands, we mapped levels of awareness and adoption of Open Research Practices (ORPs) in Ireland.  Previous evidence points to a persistent gap between researchers’ awareness and actual adoption of open practices. To investigate this phenomenon, we implemented a two-part nationwide survey of the Irish research community using the Brief Open Research Survey (BORS) to gauge levels of awareness and adoption of Open Research Practices (ORPs), and the COM-B model of behaviour change to evaluate situational and psychological drivers of adoption. Results indicate that while awareness is consistently high across open practices, adoption levels show a significant relative drop. The COM-B model analysis revealed that Motivation, the extent to which Open Research is embedded as a routine element of researchers’ workflows, was the strongest predictor of adoption, followed by Capability (having the skills and knowledge) and Opportunity (time, resources and support), corroborating previous findings. These results highlight the need for the early integration of targeted, practical training to bridge the awareness-adoption gap and to foster open practices as a habitual way of doing research.

articles.title: Epistemic disadvantage in open science practices
articles.authors: Rena Beatrice Alcalay; Joachim Vandekerckhove
articles.abstract: Open science aims to address epistemic injustice and global power asymmetries in research infrastructures yet underestimate structural vulnerabilities and inherent risks in data sharing practices. Drawing on an ethnographic study of the GCRF South-South Migration Inequality and Development Hub's (MIDEQ), we argue that even well-intentioned systems do not target vulnerabilities that equitable protocols alone cannot fix. We employ the concept of epistemic disadvantage, a category coextensive with, but separate from epistemic injustice. Epistemic disadvantage includes ethically justified data withholding and misappropriation within consensual systems.
